text.title: Mobile-First CSS Rules
text.summary: Viewport meta, mobile-first media queries, fluid typography with clamp(), responsive images, and touch targets.

rule.statement: Every HTML page must include <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> with both attributes. Missing initial-scale=1.0 breaks iOS Safari rotation.
rule.rationale: Without the viewport meta, mobile browsers render the page at desktop width and scale it down, making text unreadable without zooming.

rule.statement: Set box-sizing: border-box globally before any other styles. This makes width calculations predictable — padding and border are included in the specified width.
rule.rationale: The default content-box model causes layout breaks when borders or padding are added to percentage-width elements.

rule.statement: Use 100dvh (dynamic viewport height) instead of 100vh. On iOS Safari, 100vh includes the URL bar height, hiding bottom UI.
rule.rationale: The browser-chrome issue on iOS is the most common mobile layout bug. dvh units use the actual visible viewport.

rule.statement: Write base styles for the smallest viewport without a media query. Add min-width media queries for larger breakpoints. Never use max-width queries except for print or dark-mode overrides.
rule.rationale: Mobile-first produces additive CSS — smaller files for mobile and progressive enhancement for desktop. max-width queries produce subtractive overrides that accumulate complexity.

rule.statement: Use clamp(min, fluid, max) for font sizes and spacing. This eliminates most breakpoint-specific font rules and produces smooth scaling between viewport sizes.
rule.rationale: Stepped font sizes at breakpoints produce jarring jumps. clamp() produces continuous scaling with defined minimum and maximum bounds.

rule.statement: Every interactive element (button, link, input) must have a minimum touch target of 44x44 CSS px. This is WCAG 2.5.5 and Apple HIG minimum.
rule.rationale: Touch targets below 44px cause mis-taps on mobile. Agents translating Figma designs pixel-for-pixel routinely produce targets of 20-32px.

rule.statement: Set max-width: 100%; height: auto on all images. Use srcset + sizes for resolution switching. Use the picture element for art direction (different crops per viewport).
rule.rationale: Images without max-width overflow their container on small viewports. Without srcset, desktop-resolution images load on mobile, wasting bandwidth.
